Name: Account Discovery: Local Account
Use: OilRig has run net user, net user /domain, net group "domain admins" /domain, and net group "Exchange Trusted Subsystem" /domain to get account listings on a victim.[4]

Name: Account Discovery: Domain Account
Use: OilRig has run net user, net user /domain, net group "domain admins" /domain, and net group "Exchange Trusted Subsystem" /domain to get account listings on a victim.[4]

Name: Acquire Infrastructure: Domains
Use: OilRig has set up fake VPN portals, conference sign ups, and job application websites to target victims.[3]

Name: Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols
Use: OilRig has used HTTP for C2.[6][17][18]
During Outer Space, OilRig used HTTP to communicate between installed backdoors and compromised servers including via the Microsoft Exchange Web Services API.[16]
During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a VBS script to send POST requests to register installed malware with C2.[16]

Name: Application Layer Protocol: DNS
Use: OilRig has used DNS for C2 including the publicly available requestbin.net tunneling service.[6][17][18][10]

Name: Automated Collection
Use: OilRig has used automated collection.[6]

Name: Browser Information Discovery
Use: During Outer Space, OilRig used a Chrome data dumper named MKG.[16]
During Juicy Mix, OilRig used the CDumper (Chrome browser) and EDumper (Edge browser) data stealers to collect cookies, browsing history, and credentials.[16]

Name: Brute Force
Use: OilRig has used brute force techniques to obtain credentials.[17][12]

Name: Clipboard Data
Use: OilRig has used infostealer tools to copy clipboard data.[14]

Name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
Use: OilRig has used various types of scripting for execution.[1][19][20][7][21]

Name: PowerShell
Use: OilRig has used PowerShell scripts for execution, including use of a macro to run a PowerShell command to decode file contents.[1][22][9][13]
During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a PowerShell script to steal credentials.[16]

Name: Windows Command Shell
Use: OilRig has used macros to deliver malware such as QUADAGENT and OopsIE.[1][19][20][7][21] OilRig has used batch scripts.[1][19][20][7][21]

Name: Visual Basic
Use: OilRig has used VBScript macros for execution on compromised hosts.[10]
During Outer Space, OilRig used VBS droppers to deploy malware.[16]
During Juicy Mix, OilRig used VBS droppers to deliver and establish persistence for the Mango backdoor.[16]

Name: Compromise Accounts: Email Accounts
Use: OilRig has compromised email accounts to send phishing emails.[3]

Name: Compromise Infrastructure: Server
Use: During Outer Space, OilRig compromised an Israeli human resources site to use as a C2 server.[16]
During Juicy Mix, OilRig compromised an Israeli job portal to use for a C2 server.[16]

Name: Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service
Use: OilRig has used a compromised Domain Controller to create a service on a remote host.[14]

Name: Credentials from Password Stores
Use: OilRig has used credential dumping tools such as LaZagne to steal credentials to accounts logged into the compromised system and to Outlook Web Access.[6][17][23][18]

Name: Credentials from Web Browsers
Use: OilRig has used credential dumping tools such as LaZagne to steal credentials to accounts logged into the compromised system and to Outlook Web Access.[6][17][23][18] OilRig has also used tool named PICKPOCKET to dump passwords from web browsers.[18]
During Juicy Mix, OilRig used the CDumper (Chrome browser) and EDumper (Edge browser) to collect credentials.[16]

Name: Windows Credential Manager
Use: OilRig has used credential dumping tool named VALUEVAULT to steal credentials from the Windows Credential Manager.[18]
During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a Windows Credential Manager stealer for credential access.[16]

Name: Data Encoding: Standard Encoding
Use: During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a VBS script to send the Base64-encoded name of the compromised computer to C2.[16]

Name: Data from Local System
Use: OilRig has used PowerShell to upload files from compromised systems.[13]

Name: Data from Removable Media
Use: OilRig has used Wireshark’s usbcapcmd utility to capture USB traffic.[14]

Name: Data Staged: Local Data Staging
Use: During Juicy Mix, OilRig used browser data and credential stealer tools to stage stolen files named Cupdate, Eupdate, and IUpdate in the %TEMP% directory.[16]

Name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
Use: A OilRig macro has run a PowerShell command to decode file contents. OilRig has also used certutil to decode base64-encoded files on victims.[1][22][20][24]
During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a script to concatenate and deobfuscate encoded strings in Mango.[16]

Name: Develop Capabilities: Malware
Use: OilRig actively developed and used a series of downloaders during 2022.[25]
For Outer Space, OilRig created new implants including the Solar backdoor.[16]
For Juicy Mix, OilRig improved on Solar by developing the Mango backdoor.[16]

Name: Encrypted Channel: Asymmetric Cryptography
Use: OilRig used the PowerExchange utility and other tools to create tunnels to C2 servers.[17]

Name: Establish Accounts: Cloud Accounts
Use: During Outer Space, OilRig created M365 email accounts to be used as part of C2.[16]

Name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Use: OilRig has exfiltrated data via Microsoft Exchange and over FTP separately from its primary C2 channel over DNS.[5][13]

Name: Exploitation for Client Execution
Use: OilRig has exploited CVE-2024-30088 to run arbitrary code in the context of SYSTEM.[13]

Name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
Use: OilRig has exploited the Windows Kernel Elevation of Privilege vulnerability, CVE-2024-30088.[13]

Name: External Remote Services
Use: OilRig uses remote services such as VPN, Citrix, or OWA to persist in an environment.[17]

Name: Fallback Channels
Use: OilRig malware ISMAgent falls back to its DNS tunneling mechanism if it is unable to reach the C2 server over HTTP.[19]

Name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify System Firewall
Use: OilRig has modified Windows firewall rules to enable remote access.[14]

Name: Indicator Removal: File Deletion
Use: OilRig has deleted files associated with their payload after execution.[1][20]

Name: Ingress Tool Transfer
Use: OilRig had downloaded remote files onto victim infrastructure.[1][13]
During Outer Space, OilRig downloaded additional tools to comrpomised infrastructure.[16]

Name: Input Capture: Keylogging
Use: OilRig has employed keyloggers including KEYPUNCH and LONGWATCH.[17][18][14]

Name: Masquerading
Use: OilRig has used .doc file extensions to mask malicious executables.[10]

Name: Match Legitimate Resource Name or Location
Use: OilRig has named a downloaded copy of the Plink tunneling utility as \ProgramData\Adobe.exe.[14]

Name: Modify Authentication Process: Password Filter DLL
Use: OilRig has registered a password filter DLL in order to drop malware.[13]

Name: Modify Registry
Use: OilRig has used reg.exe to modify system configuration.[14][13]

Name: Network Service Discovery
Use: OilRig has used the publicly available tool SoftPerfect Network Scanner as well as a custom tool called GOLDIRONY to conduct network scanning.[17]

Name: Obfuscated Files or Information: Indicator Removal from Tools
Use: OilRig has tested malware samples to determine AV detection and subsequently modified the samples to ensure AV evasion.[2][21]

Name: Obfuscated Files or Information: Encrypted/Encoded File
Use: OilRig has encrypted and encoded data in its malware, including by using base64.[1][7][6][9][21]
During Outer Space, OilRig deployed VBS droppers with obfuscated strings.[16]

Name: Obtain Capabilities: Tool
Use: OilRig has made use of the publicly available tools including Plink and Mimikatz.[14][13]

Name: Obtain Capabilities: Code Signing Certificates
Use: OilRig has obtained stolen code signing certificates to digitally sign malware.[3]

Name: Office Application Startup: Outlook Home Page
Use: OilRig has abused the Outlook Home Page feature for persistence. OilRig has also used CVE-2017-11774 to roll back the initial patch designed to protect against Home Page abuse.[26]

Name: OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory
Use: OilRig has used credential dumping tools such as Mimikatz to steal credentials to accounts logged into the compromised system and to Outlook Web Access.[6][17][23][18]

Name: OS Credential Dumping: LSA Secrets
Use: OilRig has used credential dumping tools such as LaZagne to steal credentials to accounts logged into the compromised system and to Outlook Web Access.[6][17][23][18]

Name: OS Credential Dumping: Cached Domain Credentials
Use: OilRig has used credential dumping tools such as LaZagne to steal credentials to accounts logged into the compromised system and to Outlook Web Access.[6][17][23][18]

Name: Password Policy Discovery
Use: OilRig has used net.exe in a script with net accounts /domain to find the password policy of a domain.[27]

Name: Peripheral Device Discovery
Use: OilRig has used tools to identify if a mouse is connected to a targeted system.[10]

Name: Permission Groups Discovery: Local Groups
Use: OilRig has used net localgroup administrators to find local administrators on compromised systems.[4][14]

Name: Permission Groups Discovery: Domain Groups
Use: OilRig has used net group /domain, net group "domain admins" /domain, and net group "Exchange Trusted Subsystem" /domain to find domain group permission settings.[4]

Name: Phishing: Spearphishing Attachment
Use: OilRig has sent spearphising emails with malicious attachments to potential victims using compromised and/or spoofed email accounts.[20][7][9][3]

Name: Phishing: Spearphishing Link
Use: OilRig has sent spearphising emails with malicious links to potential victims.[20][3]

Name: Phishing: Spearphishing via Service
Use: OilRig has used LinkedIn to send spearphishing links.[18]

Name: Process Discovery
Use: OilRig has run tasklist on a victim's machine and used infostealers to capture processes.[4][14]

Name: Protocol Tunneling
Use: OilRig has used the Plink utility and other tools to create tunnels to C2 servers.[6][17][18][14]

Name: Query Registry
Use: OilRig has used reg query "HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Terminal Server Client\Default" on a victim to query the Registry.[4]

Name: Remote Access Tools
Use: OilRig has incorporated remote monitoring and management (RMM) tools into their operations including ngrok.[13]

Name: Remote Services: Remote Desktop Protocol
Use: OilRig has used Remote Desktop Protocol for lateral movement. The group has also used tunneling tools to tunnel RDP into the environment.[6][17][24][14][14]

Name: Remote Services: SSH
Use: OilRig has used Putty to access compromised systems.[6]

Name: Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task
Use: OilRig has created scheduled tasks that run a VBScript to execute a payload on victim machines.[20][7][18][10]
During Juicy Mix, OilRig used VBS droppers to schedule tasks for persistence.[16]

Name: Screen Capture
Use: OilRig has a tool called CANDYKING to capture a screenshot of user's desktop.[17]

Name: Server Software Component: Web Shell
Use: OilRig has used web shells, often to maintain access to a victim network.[6][17][24][13]

Name: Software Discovery
Use: During Juicy Mix, OilRig used browser data dumper tools to create a list of users with Google Chrome installed.[16]

Name: Stage Capabilities: Upload Malware
Use: OilRig has hosted malware on fake websites designed to target specific audiences.[3]

Name: Subvert Trust Controls: Code Signing
Use: OilRig has signed its malware with stolen certificates.[3]

Name: Supply Chain Compromise
Use: OilRig has leveraged compromised organizations to conduct supply chain attacks on government entities.[13]

Name: System Binary Proxy Execution: Compiled HTML File
Use: OilRig has used a CHM payload to load and execute another malicious file once delivered to a victim.[4]

Name: System Information Discovery
Use: OilRig has run hostname and systeminfo on a victim.[4][5][18][10][14]
During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a script to send the name of the compromised host via HTTP POST to register it with C2.[16]

Name: System Network Configuration Discovery
Use: OilRig has run ipconfig /all on a victim.[4][5]

Name: System Network Connections Discovery
Use: OilRig has used netstat -an on a victim to get a listing of network connections.[4]

Name: System Owner/User Discovery
Use: OilRig has run whoami on a victim.[4][5][10]

Name: System Service Discovery
Use: OilRig has used sc query on a victim to gather information about services.[4]

Name: Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files
Use: OilRig has used credential dumping tools such as LaZagne to steal credentials to accounts logged into the compromised system and to Outlook Web Access.[6][17][23][18]

Name: User Execution: Malicious Link
Use: OilRig has delivered malicious links to achieve execution on the target system.[20][7][9][3]

Name: User Execution: Malicious File
Use: OilRig has delivered macro-enabled documents that required targets to click the "enable content" button to execute the payload on the system.[20][7][9][10][3]

Tactic: Defense Evasion, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Initial Access
Name: Valid Accounts
Use: OilRig has used compromised credentials to access other systems on a victim network.[6][17][24][12]

Name: Domain Accounts
Use: OilRig has used an exfiltration tool named STEALHOOK to retreive valid domain credentials.[13]

Name: Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion: System Checks
Use: OilRig has used macros to verify if a mouse is connected to a compromised machine.[10]

Name: Windows Management Instrumentation
Use: OilRig has used WMI for execution.[17][14]
